who has the right of way in international waters Both vessels have the right of way in international waters, as there are no established priorities between them under maritime law unless specific conditions or situations dictate otherwise. This principle ensures that all ships operate on equal terms within open seas.

who has the right of way in international waters In international waters, both vessels generally have equal rights and responsibilities under maritime law unless specific conditions or circumstances dictate otherwise, making "both vessels" the correct answer. This equality ensures that no single vessel is unduly privileged or disadvantaged, promoting safe and fair navigation practices.

who has the right of way in international waters Both vessels have equal rights and responsibilities regarding the right of way in international waters, according to international maritime law as outlined in Annex I of the International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs). This means that neither vessel inherently has priority over the other without specific circumstances or conditions.

who has the right of way in international waters Both vessels have equal right of way in international waters, meaning there is no inherent precedence between them unless specific circumstances dictate otherwise under maritime law and navigation rules.
